memories.memory_text: It felt like an apparition, or some cheap, gruesome Rod Serling time warp—I was suddenly back in the past without warning. The air changed when I saw him walking in, and I could feel the heat rise between us as he stepped into the booth.
grounded_facts: Debi was broadcasting when Martin entered the station.
Debi perceived Martin's return as surreal, comparing it to a time warp or apparition.
Debi observed Martin walking back into the station from her point of view.
Martin entered the deejay booth where Debi was located.
Debi noted a shift in atmosphere, describing the temperature rising as they faced each other.

memories.memory_text: I flipped the ON-AIR switch without him seeing, and the red light bar lit up behind him—suddenly our private moment was no longer private, and I could feel the weight of the airwaves holding us both accountable.
grounded_facts: Debi secretly activated the ON-AIR switch during the conversation with Martin
The ON-AIR light bar turned on above and behind Martin
Their conversation was broadcast live over the radio
Martin was unaware at first that they were on air

memories.memory_text: After the callers spoke, I turned to him and said '...Broken....' with a shrug—my voice carried the verdict, but my body kept its distance, like I was releasing something I once held too close.
grounded_facts: Debi relayed the collective judgment of the callers using the phrase "Broken..."
She delivered the line while turning to Martin and shrugging apologetically
The moment served as the emotional conclusion of the broadcast confrontation
Physical distance was maintained after delivering the verdict

memories.memory_text: I remember the way he stood there, all polished with his car and his suit, like he was supposed to impress me—so I pointed out that he still didn't have happiness or a point of view. It felt good to level the ground between us.
grounded_facts: Debi observed Martin's appearance and outward signs of success.
Debi sarcastically noted that Martin lacked intangible qualities like happiness and character.
Martin responded with 'It's always the little things,' which Debi acknowledged with a quiet 'Yep.'

memories.memory_text: When he kept saying everything in his life was 'bad,' I couldn't help but laugh—it was so bleak and absurd—so I tossed out 'Shockabuku' like a joke, but then realized it was actually true: he needed a swift spiritual kick to the head.
grounded_facts: Martin described his past ten years as involving bad experiences, people, and television.
Debi reacted with amusement and called him pathetic.
Debi coined or referenced 'Shockabuku' as a transformative spiritual jolt in response to Martin's emptiness.

memories.memory_text: I was sitting in the deejay booth, playing that ridiculous 'would you rather' game with Martin like we used to—junkie otter, candied corpse, hot pork vest—just throwing absurdity back and forth, and for a moment, it didn’t feel like ten years had passed at all.
grounded_facts: Debi is in the deejay booth at the gym, seated and participating in a set.
She initiates and engages in a flirtatious, sophomoric 'would you rather' game with Martin.
She laughs and responds playfully, choosing 'junkie otter' and proposing outrageous hypotheticals like 'candied corpse of Phyllis Diller'.
The game is described as an old one, implying shared history and intimacy.

memories.memory_text: After Stacey came up to take over, I stood, turned to Martin, and said, 'It's time to see you in private,' then led him away—aware of the eyes on us, aware of the divorce everyone whispers about, but choosing to walk out with him anyway.
grounded_facts: Debi invites Stacey to take over the deejay booth.
She exits the booth with Martin after the interaction.
She speaks directly to Martin, saying, 'It's time to see you in private,' indicating intention and control over the moment.
She physically leads Martin toward the exit, enacting a deliberate shift from public to private space.

memories.memory_text: I walked into the room and saw photos of Mr. Newberry and me on the bed, mixed with papers—credit reports, medical records. Martin said he was the one being hunted, then admitted he worked as an assassin for about five years. I just stared at him, stunned.
grounded_facts: Debi entered Martin's suite and saw photos of Mr. Newberry, herself, and the house on the bed, along with credit reports and medical records.
Martin confirmed he was the target and stated he had worked as an assassin professionally for about five years.

memories.memory_text: After he tried to justify it—talking about moral flexibility and government programs—I told him to get out, that he was a monster. When he asked if I’d come with him or hide if he returned, I said I’d hide, then turned and told him we’d never be together because he speaks 'monster' and I speak 'human.'
grounded_facts: Debi rejected Martin’s attempt to explain his actions, calling him a monster and asserting her humanity.
She refused to go with him, stated she would hide if he returned, and declared they would not be together, citing a fundamental divide in their nature.

memories.memory_text: I saw Martin turn and point that huge gun at the front door—he fired two shots through it, the wood splitting with big holes. I was there, watching, when he bolted out toward the back.
grounded_facts: Martin drew a Desert Eagle and fired two shots through the front door
The shots left baseball-sized holes in the door
Debi was present and witnessed Martin shooting through the door
Martin left the room quickly after firing

memories.memory_text: Newberry opened the front door and I saw a man lying dead on the porch, a gun in one hand, a Fuller Brush kit in the other—then two more men climbing out of a van, guns rising fast. I pulled Newberry back inside and slammed the door just before bullets hit it.
grounded_facts: Debi saw the dead assassin on the porch with a gun and a Fuller Brush kit
Two men emerged from a van with guns
Debi pulled Newberry inside, slammed and locked the door
Bullets struck the door shortly after it was closed

memories.memory_text: I felt the weight of everything pressing down, my face still in my hands when he asked me to marry him—his voice just a whisper in my head. Without looking up, I reached out and shut the door.
grounded_facts: Debi is sitting on the edge of the tub with her face in her hands.
Martin asks Debi to marry him off-screen, heard only as voiceover.
Debi does not look up or verbally respond, but physically closes the bathroom door.

memories.memory_text: I sat beside him on the porch steps, looked at him, and said it plainly—this could never work because he kills people, and that’s something I can’t live with.
grounded_facts: Debi sits next to Martin on the front steps of the house.
Debi states that their relationship cannot work out.
Debi explicitly says, 'You kill people.'
Debi follows with 'Other people can't,' indicating her inability to accept his actions.

memories.memory_text: It felt like an apparition, or some cheap, gruesome Rod Serling time warp I'd been thrust back into without warning. There's a strangeness in the air and I don't mind telling you, I'm a little spooked. He was a man from my past. A man I loved. A man who disappeared—and now he's walking back into the station.
grounded_facts: Debi was broadcasting when Martin reentered the radio station.
Debi perceived Martin's return as surreal and disorienting, comparing it to a time warp.
Debi saw Martin enter the booth from her point of view.
Debi noted the emotional and physical intensity of the moment, including a rise in temperature as they faced each other.

memories.memory_text: I flipped the ON-AIR switch without him seeing, and when I said 'You're in therapy too, Marty?', I already knew he was on the air—his voice was no longer just for me, but for everyone listening.
grounded_facts: Debi secretly activated the ON-AIR switch during the conversation
The ON-AIR light illuminated above Martin without his knowledge
Debi asked Martin about therapy immediately after going live
The broadcast was active when she posed the question

memories.memory_text: When I put the first caller through and said 'You're on the air,' I saw him freeze—he finally understood, and there was no taking it back; their voices judged him, and I let them speak.
grounded_facts: Debi connected a caller by pressing a phone line on the console
She announced 'You're on the air' to the caller
Martin reacted with visible humiliation and deflation
Multiple callers responded to the broadcast with judgments about Martin

memories.memory_text: I was tapping a pencil to a silent rhythm, half-listening to the broadcast feed, when something prickled at the back of my neck—like being watched—but I didn’t look up.
grounded_facts: Debi was inside the radio station at night, visible through the window.
She was bored and tapping a pencil to an unheard beat.
She was unaware of Martin observing her with a rifle scope from across the street.

memories.memory_text: I was reading in the studio when he walked in—Martin, after all this time—and I remember the Talking Heads playing softly, that familiar voice filling the space between us as I looked up and saw him standing there, not half bad, like nothing had changed.
grounded_facts: Debi was reading when Martin entered the radio station.
The song 'Naive Melody' by Talking Heads was playing during the encounter.
Martin approached Debi and asked if she was going to the reunion.

memories.memory_text: He actually asked me out—just like that—after ten years of silence, and I couldn’t tell if I wanted to laugh or walk away; I said 'I’ll think about it,' because even though it felt absurd, something in me didn’t want to shut the door completely.
grounded_facts: Martin asked Debi out on a date, proposing to pick her up at seven.
Debi responded with disbelief, calling the moment 'unbelievable'.
She concluded the exchange with 'I'll think about it.'

memories.memory_text: I opened the door to find Martin standing there with flowers, and I couldn't help but say, 'Flowers. That's funny.' I took them from him, half-joking, and told him I'd put them in rubbing alcohol—then stepped back inside, letting him follow.
grounded_facts: Martin arrived at Debi's house holding a bouquet of flowers.
Debi answered the door and made a sarcastic remark about the flowers.
Debi took the flowers and suggested putting them in rubbing alcohol.
Debi backed into the house and Martin followed her inside.

memories.memory_text: Martin told me I looked beautiful, and I remember saying I’d be right back to get my coat—just a small moment, but I could feel the weight of him watching me as I turned toward the stairs.
grounded_facts: Martin complimented Debi by saying 'You look beautiful.'
Debi responded with 'Okay... Hold on...' and then said (off-screen) '...Let me get my coat.'
Debi moved up the stairs and left the foyer, physically distancing herself from Martin and her father's presence.

memories.memory_text: Martin was breathing hard, wiping his palms on his pants like he was about to face a firing squad, and when I asked what was wrong, he just muttered something about wishing he'd brought his gun.
grounded_facts: Martin exited the car and leaned against it while visibly nervous, breathing deeply and wiping sweaty palms.
Debi heard Martin mutter 'Shoulda brought my gun' in a low voice, prompting her to ask 'What?'
Martin did not answer her question and instead began walking toward the school.

memories.memory_text: I was sitting in the deejay booth, tossing ridiculous questions at Martin like we used to—junkie otter or candied Phyllis Diller, hot pork vest or crab apple in your mouth—just to see if he’d still play along after all this time.
grounded_facts: Debi initiated and participated in a flirtatious, absurd game with Martin in the deejay booth
The game involved outlandish hypothetical choices, including the junkie otter and candied corpse of Phyllis Diller
Debi laughed and engaged playfully, maintaining control of the rhythm and pace of the exchange

memories.memory_text: After Stacey interrupted with that awkward comment about my divorce, I didn’t let it land—I just handed her the booth and told Martin it was time to see him in private, pulling him away from everyone else’s eyes.
grounded_facts: Stacey expressed surprise at seeing Debi and Martin together, referencing Debi’s divorce
Debi dismissed Stacey’s discomfort by offering her the deejay position
Debi physically led Martin away from the booth after saying, 'It's time to see you in private'

memories.memory_text: I smiled and said 'Like heroin?' just to tease him, to see how he'd twist it—his words always had that heavy, poetic weight, and I liked watching him squirm a little under the cheapness of it.
grounded_facts: Debi said 'Like heroin?' in a smiling, playful manner during a conversation with Martin in the school hallway.
The comment was a response to Martin's statement about her being a substance in his blood.

memories.memory_text: I stopped walking, said 'I'll accept that,' and told him to follow me—I remember the weight of that decision, stepping forward into the Nurse's Office, closing the door behind us.
grounded_facts: Debi stopped moving in the hallway and said 'I'll accept that. Follow me.'
She led Martin into the Nurse's Office and they closed the door together.

memories.memory_text: I kissed Martin as the song started playing, then told him I'd be right back—something about that music meant I had to act.
grounded_facts: Debi kissed Martin after hearing 'Don't You Want Me, Baby?' from the gym.
Debi said 'I'll be right back' before leaving Martin.
The song triggered a shift in Debi's mood, prompting her decision to intervene.

memories.memory_text: I saw Martin spin and drive a pen into Felix’s throat—blood everywhere—and when he turned and locked eyes with me, I ran before he could do the same to me.
grounded_facts: Debi witnessed Martin killing Felix with a pen to the throat in the hallway.
Debi made eye contact with Martin immediately after the killing, before fleeing.
Debi perceived the blood, the impalement, and Martin’s sudden, violent movements as immediate threats.

memories.memory_text: I walked in and saw Martin had dropped the gun. I asked if Newberry was trying to kill him, not the other way around.
grounded_facts: Debi entered Martin's suite after Martin had already opened the door and dropped the gun.
She observed Martin's relaxed posture and the gun on the bed before speaking.
She asked 'Not the other way around...?' after Martin confirmed Newberry tried to kill him.

memories.memory_text: After he admitted to being a killer, not by accident but as a job, I told him to get out, and when he asked if I’d come with him, I said no—and at the door, I made it clear: he was a monster and I was human, and that was the end of it.
grounded_facts: Martin admitted to working in 'mechanical operations' for five years as part of a CIA-sponsored program.
Debi responded with 'Get the fuck outta here' after learning about his profession.
She rejected his offer to leave together, stated she would stay, and declared he was a monster while asserting her humanity before leaving.

memories.memory_text: I saw the front door after two shots were fired through it. When Newberry opened the door, I saw a dead man on the porch holding a gun and a Fuller Brush kit.
grounded_facts: Two shots were fired through the front door, creating baseball-sized holes.
Debi saw the assassin's body on the porch after the door was opened.
The body was holding a gun and a Fuller Brush kit.

memories.memory_text: Right after saying he loved me and vanished on prom night because he wanted to kill someone, Martin turned and fired through the door—his words and the gunfire hit me at the same time.
grounded_facts: Martin declared his love and explained he avoided Debi in 1984 because he felt an urge to kill.
He immediately pointed the gun at the door and fired two shots.
Debi was present and witnessed both the confession and the shooting.

memories.memory_text: Martin yelled he was in love with me while rushing the assassin with a skillet. He hit the assassin and then stood there, spattered with blood, looking at me.
grounded_facts: Martin declared his love for Debi during the attack.
Martin used a skillet to strike the assassin.
Debi saw Martin after the attack, spattered with blood, looking at her.

memories.memory_text: Martin said he just needed time to change while leading us upstairs. He shot an assassin coming up the stairs, blowing his arms off. He said he wasn’t raised in a loving environment, but that it wasn’t an excuse—just a reason.
grounded_facts: Martin said 'I just need time to change' while leading Debi and Newberry up the stairs.
Martin shot an assassin coming up the stairs, severing his arms.
Martin stated he wasn't raised in a loving environment and that it was a reason, not an excuse.
Debi witnessed Martin speaking and acting during their movement from the kitchen to the bedroom.

memories.memory_text: I heard his voice asking me to marry him, but I couldn't look up—I just reached out and closed the door.
grounded_facts: Martin asked Debi to marry her while she was sitting on the edge of the tub with her face in her hands.
Debi did not respond verbally or look up when the proposal was made.
Debi closed the bathroom door without speaking or making eye contact.

memories.memory_text: I sat beside him on the porch, looked at him, and said it plainly—this will never work out. I told him, 'You kill people,' and meant it not as an accusation but as a fact I could no longer live with.
grounded_facts: Debi sits next to Martin on the front steps of the house.
Debi says 'This will never work out' and 'You kill people' to Martin.
Debi observes Martin's reaction and shares a moment of quiet understanding, ending in a shared laugh.

memories.memory_text: I told Martin it was time to evolve—like a club, work less, make more, and he’d be in on the ground floor if he had the guts. He didn’t hang up right away, so he heard every word.
grounded_facts: Grocer proposed forming a professional collective for elite assassins during a phone call with Martin.
He used the phrases 'like a club', 'work less, make more', and 'making big money, killing important people'.
He referenced their past working relationship and attempted to appeal to Martin personally.
He ended the conversation with the threat 'I'm gonna get you, kid.'

memories.memory_text: I sat at my oak desk, clicked through the remote access, and pushed the $560,000 wire for Detroit—watched the confirmation number pop up, AJ6687-OI99471, then toasted the screen with my juice glass.
grounded_facts: Grocer used a computer at an antique oak desk to initiate a wire transfer.
The transfer was for $560,000 with availability requested for two days in the Detroit area.
The confirmation number AJ6687-OI99471 appeared on screen after the transfer.
Grocer acknowledged the confirmation by hitting the return button and toasting the computer with his juice glass.

memories.memory_text: I moved into the diner, aware they were watching me—Lardner and McCullers—cutting their little joke short just by my presence.
grounded_facts: The Grocer enters the diner during Lardner and McCullers' stakeout.
His arrival interrupts their conversation and laughter.
He is seen from their point of view as he moves into the diner.

memories.memory_text: I slid into the booth across from Martin, hands already moving toward my piece the second he drew—our eyes locked, guns under the table, breakfast order about to be taken like we weren't seconds from blowing each other’s brains out.
grounded_facts: Grocer enters the diner and sits across from Martin.
Martin draws a pistol under the table, but Grocer is already drawing his own.
An armed standoff occurs while both men remain seated in the booth.
The waitress approaches to take their order during the standoff.

memories.memory_text: I tried to smooth things over—apologized for yesterday, said it was a misunderstanding, but when he shot down the idea of working together, I snapped, told him I’d shoot him in the forehead and fuck the hole, couldn’t let that disrespect stand.
grounded_facts: Grocer apologizes for the previous day's incident, calling it a misunderstanding.
Grocer mentions he ordered Martin's death, but his associates failed to carry it out.
Martin rejects the idea of partnership, emphasizing his lone status.
Grocer escalates verbally with a violent threat after emotional distress.

memories.memory_text: I had the team ready—nine of us pulled off the highway, grabbed our guns from the kits, and started loading up. This was no longer about hiding; it was time to move.
grounded_facts: Grocer is present on a Michigan highway during the morning.
Grocer has assembled a team of approximately nine people.
The group exits a vehicle and retrieves firearms from kit bags.
The team begins loading their weapons.

memories.memory_text: I watched Newberry jogging toward us, just a speck in the distance, while the sniper got ready to take the shot.
grounded_facts: Grocer is present in the van with Company, observing Newberry from a distance.
Newberry is jogging toward the van's location.
A sniper is preparing to shoot Newberry.

memories.memory_text: I saw Martin's car coming in hot, speeding straight at Newberry — that's when I knew things were about to go loud.
grounded_facts: Grocer observes Martin's car speeding toward Newberry from inside the van.
Grocer reacts verbally with 'Oh shit...'

memories.memory_text: I saw Martin's car vanish down that long, tree-lined driveway—knew right then he wasn't just another hitter passing through. Either he's soft now, or he's changed. Either way, we move.
grounded_facts: Grocer observed Martin's car entering Newberry's wooded driveway from inside the van.
Grocer interpreted Martin's action as a sign of emotional involvement or moral shift.
Grocer decided to take immediate action after making this observation.

memories.memory_text: I shifted the van into gear and started moving toward the driveway, keeping my eyes sharp for any threats as I advanced.
grounded_facts: Grocer drops the van into gear.
Grocer heads toward the driveway.

memories.memory_text: I called out from the vent, my voice muffled, telling Blank not to listen to Martin—he's just a professional. I said it was breaking my heart, that I couldn't even shoot through the tears.
grounded_facts: Grocer speaks from off-screen, his voice coming from a vent in the bedroom.
Grocer addresses Martin as 'Blank' and expresses emotional distress.
Grocer states, 'You're breaking my heart down here, Blank. I can't shoot through the tears.'
Grocer warns Debi and Newberry not to believe Martin, calling him a professional.

memories.memory_text: I heard Lardner and McCullers bust through the front door, shooting low, so I spun and fired with Martin—our guns lit them up fast. Then I dove off the stairs and rolled, already moving toward the kitchen.
grounded_facts: Grocer heard Lardner and McCullers enter the foyer and open fire.
Grocer and Martin simultaneously fired on Lardner and McCullers, killing them.
Grocer immediately dove off the stairs and moved out of view after the shooting.

memories.memory_text: I offered to sell Martin two bullets for a hundred grand each—he tossed a balled-up check at me, bounced off my head. As I arced the rounds through the air, he lunged at the TV, smashed it into my head hard enough to snap my neck.
grounded_facts: Grocer proposed selling two bullets to Martin for a hundred thousand dollars each.
Martin threw a crumpled check that hit Grocer in the head and landed in his lap.
Grocer tossed two bullets into the air while rising and cocking his weapon.
Martin used the television to attack Grocer, resulting in Grocer's death.

memories.memory_text: I was driving alongside Martin on the FDR, phone in hand, and watched him realize it was me—Doorman, Grocer, the same man he thought he knew. I could see the shift in his face, that flicker of recognition, before he even said a word.
grounded_facts: Grocer is revealed to be the Doorman while driving a Town Car next to Martin.
Grocer initiates a phone call with Martin while maintaining visual contact through the cars' windows.

memories.memory_text: I told him about the club—work less, make more—and offered him a spot at the start. He mocked me, refused, called it a dirty guild, but I made it clear: I'm coming for him anyway.
grounded_facts: Grocer proposes an exclusive organization for assassins to consolidate work and increase profits.
Grocer states 'I'm gonna get you, kid' after Martin rejects the offer.

memories.memory_text: I finished pushing the beets through the juicer, the machine humming under my hands, and poured the last of the juice into the glass before heading out of the kitchen.
grounded_facts: Grocer is wearing a burgundy Fila sweat suit.
Grocer is using a vegetable juicer to process beets, celery, and carrots.
Track lighting illuminates the kitchen.
A low-key BLIP sound occurs from another room during the scene.
Grocer tops off a glass with juice and leaves the kitchen.

memories.memory_text: I entered the terms—$560,000, two days in Detroit, remote access—and hit OK; the confirmation number AJ6687-OI99471 flashed on the screen, and I tapped return, then raised my juice in a toast to the machine.
grounded_facts: Grocer sits at a computer on an antique oak desk and initiates a remote access transaction.
The on-screen prompt displays 'Availability for two days in Detroit area', '$560,000', and 'Now' as terms.
After seeing the confirmation number 'AJ6687-OI99471', Grocer hits the return button.
The fax machine activates and the screen shows 'Connection is terminated/ Status idle'.
Grocer raises his juice glass in a toast toward the computer.

memories.memory_text: I slid into the booth and felt Martin go for his piece under the table, but I was already drawing mine—my hand was faster, and we locked eyes with guns aimed beneath the table.
grounded_facts: Grocer enters the diner and sits across from Martin.
Martin draws a pistol under the table.
Grocer is already drawing his own pistol in response.
An armed stand-off begins beneath the table while remaining hidden from the waitress.

memories.memory_text: After he said no, I told him I'd shoot him through the forehead and fuck him in the bullethole. He just said, 'Nice talk, Sugarmouth.'
grounded_facts: Grocer delivers the threat: 'after I shoot you through the fucking forehead I'm gonna fuck you in the bullethole' after Martin refuses to join.
Martin responds with 'Nice talk, Sugarmouth.'

memories.memory_text: I had the team of nine ready, guns drawn from the kit bags as we hit the exit ramp—everyone moving just like I planned.
grounded_facts: Grocer is present on the Michigan highway during the morning.
Grocer has assembled a team of about nine people.
The group is exiting from a vehicle onto a highway ramp.
Team members are drawing and loading guns from kit bags.

memories.memory_text: I'm in the van with the others, watching Newberry jog toward us. A sniper is preparing to shoot him.
grounded_facts: Grocer is in the van with others, observing Newberry approaching.
Newberry is jogging toward their location.
A sniper is preparing to shoot Newberry.

memories.memory_text: I saw Martin's car coming fast down Newberry—just a split second of motion before I knew it was wrong. My gut said trouble, and I said, 'Oh shit...'
grounded_facts: Grocer observes Martin's car speeding on Newberry.
Grocer reacts verbally with 'Oh shit...'

memories.memory_text: I watched Martin's car vanish down that long, tree-lined driveway, and it hit me—he’s either got a death wish or something big’s going down. I said, 'That punk is either in love with that guy's daughter or he has new found respect for life... Let's go.'
grounded_facts: Grocer observed Martin's car entering Newberry's secluded, wooded driveway.
Grocer expressed skepticism or speculation about Martin's motives, referencing love for the daughter or a change in attitude toward life.
Grocer immediately decided to follow, signaling action with 'Let's go.'

memories.memory_text: I started moving in through the door, just beginning to cross the threshold, when gunfire erupted—saw the muzzle flash, heard the rounds rip through the air, and I bailed back out fast.
grounded_facts: Grocer began entering the foyer as Martin was on the stairs.
Martin opened fire with a twelve-round clip as Grocer was entering.
Grocer immediately retreated from the doorway under fire.

memories.memory_text: I was running across the kitchen, firing at Martin while vaulting through the air toward the serving window—he was pinned behind the island, shooting back as I moved.
grounded_facts: Grocer runs a pattern across the kitchen while firing at Martin.
Martin dives behind the cooking island and returns fire.
Grocer vaults through the air toward a serving window while still firing.

memories.memory_text: I offered Martin two bullets for a hundred grand each, tossed the rounds into the air—then started to rise with my gun cocked, just before he slammed that TV into my head.
grounded_facts: Grocer proposes a deal to sell two rounds for a hundred thousand dollars each.
Martin tosses a balled check over the counter, which hits Grocer.
Grocer removes two bullets from his clip and throws them into the air.
Grocer begins to rise and cock his gun before Martin attacks with the television.

memories.memory_text: I told the driver I've lost my passion for work and that I work alone—he didn't judge, just nodded like he understood, maybe because he's alone too, sitting in that car hour after hour.
grounded_facts: Martin states he has lost his passion for work.
Martin says he works alone.
The driver responds by relating his own isolation, implying understanding of solitary work.

memories.memory_text: I was closing my bag when the sound of gunfire ripped through the air—I grabbed my scope, spun to the window, and saw the Doorman kicking through the bodies, then firing both revolvers point-blank at the boss.
grounded_facts: Martin hears gunshots while closing his bag. Martin uses a scope to observe the Doorman shooting the boss after clearing bodyguards. Martin is surprised by the presence of another shooter. Martin reacts by pulling away from the window and preparing to leave. The observation occurs from Martin's point of view through a scope. The Doorman uses two chrome .44 Magnum revolvers. The Doorman fires both guns at the boss lying at the bottom of a pile of bodyguards. The Doorman kicks through the pile of dead bodyguards to reach the boss. Martin drops the phone and retrieves the scope after hearing gunfire. Martin accelerates his exit after witnessing the hit. The scene takes place near or in a high-rise building with a view of the street. The gunfire happens shortly after Martin finishes packing. The Doorman is described as tall
dark
sharp-featured
in his forties

with a handlebar mustache. The Doorman unbuttons his coat to reveal the revolvers before opening fire. The two bodyguards kick the Messenger's body before the Doorman arrives. The other two bodyguards begin to move off the boss before the Doorman starts shooting. The Doorman stands behind the group before moving in. The Doorman unbuttons his double-breasted long coat before flipping it open. The Doorman executes the hit in a public street setting. The Doorman uses a methodical

controlled approach during the hit. Martin witnesses the entire sequence through the scope. The Doorman's actions are violent and efficient. The Doorman finishes the hit by shooting both guns at close range. The Doorman drops both guns

memories.memory_text: I told Grocer 'thank you, no' when he offered me a spot in his consortium—like it was some kind of club, some cozy setup where we'd work less and make more. I wasn't interested in his version of teamwork.
grounded_facts: Martin explicitly rejects Grocer's offer to join a professional consortium.
The offer is framed as a business opportunity with reduced workload and increased profit.
Martin uses a polite but firm refusal: 'Thank you, no.'

memories.memory_text: I asked if he wanted to be a father figure to me, right after he called me 'kid'—it came out dry, maybe a little sharp, but I said it straight, watching his car in the lane beside mine.
grounded_facts: Martin responds to Grocer calling him 'kid' with a sarcastic remark about father figures.
The comment is delivered verbally during a phone conversation while both are in separate cars on FDR Drive.
The remark is not acknowledged by Grocer, who continues speaking.

memories.memory_text: I saw that mauve envelope and felt a jolt—Marcella smiling, holding it out like it was nothing. I told her to throw it away, not to tease me, and meant it.
grounded_facts: Marcella presented Martin with a high school reunion invitation in a mauve envelope.
Martin visibly reacted with fear upon seeing the invitation.
Martin instructed Marcella to discard the invitation and never show him personal mail again.

memories.memory_text: They wanted me in Detroit this weekend—no prep, no time. I said it was impossible, but Marcella said their position was intractable. I leave tonight.
grounded_facts: Martin was informed of an urgent job assignment in Detroit scheduled for that weekend.
Martin objected due to lack of his normal lead time.
Marcella conveyed that the clients were insistent and their position was inflexible, requiring Martin to leave immediately.

memories.memory_text: I found her picture in the yearbook—Debi Newbery—older, smiling, just like that. For a second, everything else in the room faded, even the cat’s meowing.
grounded_facts: Martin finds a yearbook in a cardboard box after feeding his cat.
He sees Debi Newbery's senior picture in the yearbook.
The picture shows a more mature version of the girl from earlier photos.
Her name appears under the picture.

memories.memory_text: I stood at the payphone, staring at the 7-11 where my house used to be, and told Dr. Oatman my home was gone—replaced by this fluorescent store, and all I could say afterward was that at least you can shop there.
grounded_facts: Martin called Dr. Oatman from a payphone outside a 7-11.
He reported that his childhood house was gone and replaced by the 7-11.
He hung up and muttered to himself, 'But I guess you can shop there.'
He observed shoppers entering and leaving the store after the call.

memories.memory_text: I told my mother I’ve always thought about Debi and missed her, standing there by the car, the words coming out quieter than I meant them to.
grounded_facts: Martin says, 'I have always thought about her and missed her.'
This occurs during a conversation with his mother, Mary, outside the nursing home, after mentioning he might go see Debi.
The statement follows his admission that he doesn’t know what to say to Debi after leaving without goodbye.

memories.memory_text: After I kissed her and the nurse started to wheel her away, she turned back, sang that old tune like she was on a stage, and then—just like that—looked at me like I was a stranger, asking my name.
grounded_facts: Martin kisses his mother on the cheek.
The nurse begins wheeling Mary away.
Martin calls out 'Mom,' prompting the nurse to turn Mary around.
Mary sings a Broadway-style song directed at Martin.
After singing, Mary squints at Martin and says, 'Hey, you're a handsome devil. What's your name?'
This indicates she no longer recognizes him.

memories.memory_text: I walked in and said it was nice to see her again, but she just stared—frozen, like I’d ripped something open she’d sealed shut.
grounded_facts: Martin entered the radio station and greeted Debi.
Debi did not respond verbally, only stared at him in shock.
Martin remembers her silence and the weight of her gaze.

memories.memory_text: I told her I was a professional killer, and she asked if I got dental—flat, dry, like she couldn’t, or wouldn’t, let it in.
grounded_facts: Martin stated he was a professional killer when asked about his business.
Debi responded with a sarcastic question about dental benefits.
The exchange was marked by a beat of silence, indicating emotional tension.

memories.memory_text: I saw Felix walking down the street—bookish, forgettable, but I knew him. The way he looked at Lardner and McCullers, and how they looked back—no words, but I felt it. Something cold. A signal. I didn't like what came up.
grounded_facts: Martin sees Felix and recognizes him from the past. Martin observes Felix's glance toward Lardner and McCullers. Martin notices the silent eye contact between Felix
Lardner
and McCullers. Martin feels a sense of threat or unease based on the interaction. Martin remains alert and suspicious during this moment.

memories.memory_text: Then this guy in sunglasses walks straight at me, hand going into his jacket—thought he was drawing a gun. Turned out to be Paul. Took a second to register. He was grinning, holding out his hand, calling me 'Marty.' I didn't recognize him at first. We shook. He looked... normal. Too normal.
grounded_facts: A man approaches Martin with his hand in his breast pocket. Martin assumes the man is reaching for a weapon. The man removes sunglasses and is revealed to be Paul Swiderski. Paul greets Martin warmly and extends his hand. Martin does not immediately recognize Paul. They eventually shake hands after Martin realizes who Paul is. Martin is surprised by Paul's appearance and demeanor.

memories.memory_text: I asked the guard when he was authorized to use deadly force, and he confirmed he could shoot me under heightened circumstances—not as a peace officer, but under company policy. His badge wasn't a symbol of law, just of corporate authority.
grounded_facts: Martin questions the Southtec guard about the conditions under which he is authorized to use deadly force.
The guard responds that he can shoot someone like Martin if they are on the property under 'heightened circumstances'.
Martin observes that the guard's badge does not represent legal authority but corporate policy.
The exchange highlights a parallel between the guard's sanctioned use of force and Martin's own role as a hired killer.

memories.memory_text: I told them I wanted a beautiful, normal place like that house, and a wife like the woman there—something real. Then I gestured to the guard and said they'd be safe, but everyone just looked confused, and Paul stared at his shoes.
grounded_facts: Martin expresses a sincere desire for a normal life, including a home like the Frank Lloyd Wright house and a wife like the prospective buyer.
He directs the comment toward the couple and the guard, saying 'you'll be safe here'.
His statement is met with confusion, and Paul responds nonverbally by looking at his shoes and shifting uncomfortably.
The moment stands out as emotionally vulnerable compared to Martin's usual detachment.

memories.memory_text: I stood across the street, watching Debi through the scope—she was just there, tapping a pencil like nothing mattered. I lowered the scope and got in the car instead.
grounded_facts: Martin stands across the street from the radio station and observes Debi in the window.
He takes out a rifle scope, looks through it, and sees Debi in the crosshairs.
He puts the scope away and gets in his car without taking any further action.

memories.memory_text: I was crouched behind the Slurpee machine, reloading both Glocks, when Felix's gunfire hit it and sprayed me with cold syrup—cherry, lime, who knows. I remember the stickiness on my face right before I came up shooting.
grounded_facts: Martin takes cover behind the Slurpee machine after being fired upon by Felix
He reloads two Glock 9mm pistols while pinned down
The Slurpee machine is hit by gunfire
causing flavored liquid to splatter on him
He retaliates with both guns after the machine is hit

memories.memory_text: I rolled Carl over, saw the bomb strapped underneath him, and flipped him back fast. I grabbed the kid from the game, but he shrugged me off like I was interrupting something important—right before his screen blew out and I yanked him through the doors.
grounded_facts: Martin checks Carl's body and discovers a bomb beneath it
He immediately flips Carl back onto the bomb
He attempts to pull the skateboarder to safety
The skateboarder resists
reacting with annoyance
Martin's gunfire destroys the video game screen just before pulling the skateboarder out

memories.memory_text: I told Debi the last ten years were just bad experiences, bad people, bad television—each word felt like pulling a trigger with no bullet left.
grounded_facts: Martin responds to Debi's prompts about his life by saying 'Bad experiences,' 'Bad people,' and 'Bad television.'
The exchange occurs during a quiet, reflective moment outside the radio station at night.
Martin's tone is flat and self-deprecating, consistent with his detached and pragmatic demeanor.

memories.memory_text: After all that emptiness, I asked her out—just said I'd pick her up at seven, like it was a job to be scheduled.
grounded_facts: Martin proposes to pick Debi up 'tomorrow around seven o'clock' after a pause in the conversation.
Debi interprets this as a date and confirms, 'Are you asking me out?' to which Martin replies, 'Yes.'
The moment follows a beat of silence, indicating deliberation before the request.

memories.memory_text: I picked up the envelope and was about to open it when the phone rang. Debi told me to pick her up at her father's house at seven and not be late. I dropped the envelope and answered.
grounded_facts: Martin picked up the dossier and was about to open it when the phone rang.
Debi called and told Martin to pick her up at her father's house at seven.
Martin dropped the envelope and answered the phone.

memories.memory_text: After she hung up, I smiled—really smiled—and looked back at the guns and bullets spread on the bed. Then I closed the briefcase, put the dossier inside, and shoved it all back into the vent.
grounded_facts: Martin smiled after ending the call with Debi.
He looked at the weapons and equipment on the bed.
He placed the unopened dossier into the briefcase, closed it, and returned it to the air vent.

memories.memory_text: I saw a pair of legs in a recliner through the doorway—Debi’s father, I assumed—and something about it made me roll my shoulders before walking in. I said I’d help myself to a cocktail, like it was normal, like I belonged there.
grounded_facts: Martin sees only the legs of Mr. Newberry in a Barcolounger from his point of view.
Martin rolls his shoulders after looking at the legs.
Martin says he will help himself to a cocktail while Debi goes upstairs.

memories.memory_text: I told Mr. Newberry I took the other road—that I'm a self-reflective young lion who does business with lead-pipe cruelty and drinks light beer in milky-eyes isolation, loving sports and sex but no real relationships.
grounded_facts: Martin describes himself as a 'self-reflective young lion' who operates with 'lead-pipe cruelty'.
Martin states he goes home to drink light beer in 'milky-eyes isolation'.
Martin says he loves sports and sex but has no real relationships.

memories.memory_text: When he asked what I’m doing now, I said, 'I'm a professional killer,' and he just said, 'That's good,' which felt absurd, but I didn’t react.
grounded_facts: Martin states his profession directly: 'I'm a professional killer.'
Mr. Newberry responds with 'That's good,' without visible shock or disbelief.
Martin does not react to the response, maintaining composure.

memories.memory_text: I was on the phone, standing by the Town Car, and I said it out loud—I didn't kill anyone, but if I see the guy who tried to kill me, I'm going to kill him. I made it clear I wouldn't kill anyone else, except the one I was sent here for. I needed to set that line.
grounded_facts: Martin was standing at a kiosk next to a Town Car during a phone conversation.
He stated that someone tried to kill him and that an innocent man died as a result.
He declared his intent to kill the person who tried to kill him, but no one else, except his original target.
He expressed uncertainty about the situation by saying 'I don't know...'

memories.memory_text: I was standing by the car, trying to steady my breath, hands slick with sweat—I muttered to myself I should’ve brought my gun, like it could fix the knot in my chest.
grounded_facts: Martin pulls into the high school parking lot with Debi.
Martin breathes deeply and wipes his sweaty palms.
Martin leans against the car to compose himself.
Martin says, 'Shoulda brought my gun,' under his breath.
Eighties music plays from the gym, adding to the atmosphere.

memories.memory_text: I told Debi she was in my blood, not just memory—something deeper than junk, something like love, though I wouldn’t name it that. She smiled and said she’d accept that, then led me into the nurse’s office.
grounded_facts: Martin says Debi never left him, not just as memory but as a substance in his blood.
Debi compares it to heroin, which Martin dismisses as too kitschy.
Debi suggests 'like love,' and Martin considers it possible.
Debi stops, accepts his words, and says 'Follow me,' leading him into the nurse’s office.

memories.memory_text: I told Debi the truth—about being a hitman, about five years of doing it professionally—and when she called me a monster, I didn’t argue. I just started cleaning my guns.
grounded_facts: Martin admits to Debi that he is a hitman and has been doing it professionally for about five years.
After Debi leaves, Martin silently picks up a cleaning rag and begins cleaning his weapons.
Martin does not respond defensively after being called a monster, but instead engages in a routine, methodical action.

memories.memory_text: I roared toward Newberry, engine screaming, closing the distance—yards, then feet—before I swerved hard at the last second, tires screeching, coming to a stop beside him, blocking the sniper’s shot.
grounded_facts: Martin drives directly at Newberry with increasing speed.
At close range, Martin swerves and stops next to Newberry.
The maneuver blocks the sniper's line of fire.

memories.memory_text: After he got in, I told him I was hired to kill him—but I wasn’t going to do it. I didn’t know if it was because I loved his daughter or if something in me had changed, but I knew I had to get him out of there.
grounded_facts: Martin admits to Newberry that he was hired to kill him.
Martin states he will not carry out the assassination.
Martin offers two possible reasons: love for Newberry's daughter or a new respect for life.
Newberry enters the car after the confrontation.

memories.memory_text: I told Amy I work at Kentucky Fried Chicken, in the corporate offices—just to see if she’d believe it, to push the lie far enough that I wouldn’t have to say what I really do.
grounded_facts: Martin says 'I work at Kentucky Fried Chicken.'
Martin clarifies he works in the corporate offices when questioned.

memories.memory_text: I kept going—telling her I sell biscuits to the Southland, even the horsey gravy packets, and that we sometimes repackage them for McDonald's—each word more absurd, but it was easier than silence.
grounded_facts: Martin says 'I sell biscuits to the Southland.'
Martin mentions selling horsey biscuit gravy packets and repackaging them as barbecue sauce for McDonald's.

memories.memory_text: I told Debi she’s not just a memory, but a substance in my blood—something real, physical, deeper than junk or clichés.
grounded_facts: Martin says Debi never left him, not just in memory but as a substance in his blood.
He rejects 'like heroin' as too kitschy, insisting it's deeper.
He accepts 'like love' as a possible explanation, framing it as a physical substance.

memories.memory_text: She said she’d accept that, then told me to follow her—so I did, into the Nurse’s Office, and we closed the door behind us.
grounded_facts: Debi says 'I'll accept that' and tells Martin to follow her.
Martin follows Debi into the Nurse's Office.
They close the door behind them.

memories.memory_text: I told Debi it was something I do professionally, about five years now—my voice flat, like stating the weather—while the dossier lay scattered on the bed, photos of Newberry and her mixed in with medical records and credit reports.
grounded_facts: Martin confesses to Debi that being a hitman is his profession
which he has done for about five years. The contents of the dossier
including photos and personal records of Newberry and Debi
are spread on the bed. Martin speaks in a detached
matter-of-fact tone during the confession.

memories.memory_text: She called me a monster, said I speak monster and she speaks human, then walked out—her voice sharp and final—leaving me alone with the guns on the bed, the weight of it all settling in as I started cleaning them without thinking.
grounded_facts: Debi rejects Martin
calling him a monster and stating they cannot be together. She explicitly contrasts her humanity with his perceived monstrosity. After she leaves
Martin remains in the room and begins mechanically cleaning his weapons.
